title: Chocolate chip cookies
subtitle: American-style: crisp edge, soft centre
excerpt: Twenty-four cookies with crisp, slightly browned edges and a soft, fudgy centre. Brown butter and extra yolk give more flavour than the basic recipe; resting the dough overnight deepens the chocolate.
sourceNotes: The American chocolate chip cookie derives from the Toll House recipe (1930s). The brown-butter technique is documented in mid-twentieth-century American baking literature. This recipe follows the standard US home-baking formula adapted for British ingredients: plain flour in place of all-purpose, caster sugar in place of granulated, dark chocolate chips.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked dough balls on a tray, then bag. Bake from frozen adding 2–3 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough improves with an overnight rest in the fridge. Rest up to 72 hours for deeper flavour.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; bake one tray at a time for even results

glossaryTerms.definition: Butter cooked over medium heat until the milk solids turn golden-brown and smell nutty. The Maillard reaction that turns them creates hundreds of flavour compounds that plain melted butter lacks.

content.text: The basic American cookie formula works. The version here makes two small changes: brown the butter, and add an extra egg yolk. Both moves are about flavour. The
adds a toasty, almost caramel note that plain melted butter cannot. The extra yolk adds richness without making the cookie cakey.
One American cup of all-purpose flour is approximately 125 g. This recipe uses 280 g of plain flour — the British equivalent of 2¼ cups.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a small saucepan over medium heat. Keep cooking, swirling occasionally, until the foam subsides and the milk solids at the bottom of the pan turn a golden hazelnut brown — about 5–7 minutes. The smell should shift from plain butter to something nutty and slightly caramelised. Pour into a large bowl immediately; leave the darkest solids in the pan. Leave to cool for 15 minutes.

Whisk the {{dark-brown-sugar}} and {{caster-sugar}} into the cooled browned butter until smooth. Add the egg, extra yolk, and {{vanilla-extract}} and beat for 1 minute until the mixture lightens slightly. Stir in the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} until a dough forms. Fold in the {{dark-chocolate-chips}}.

Cover the bowl and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, and up to 72 hours. The rest allows the flour to hydrate fully and gives the sugar time to draw moisture from the dough, producing a cookie that spreads less and tastes more complex.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Line the baking tray with baking paper. Scoop the dough into balls roughly 45 g each — about the size of a golf ball. Space them 5 cm apart. Bake one tray at a time for 11–12 minutes. The cookies are done when the edges are set and golden-brown and the centres still look underdone and slightly shiny. They firm up as they cool.
Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before moving to a rack. They need to set before you handle them.
